title: GitHub Copilot CLI Fundamentals
course: Agentic Development with GitHub Copilot CLI, LakeBank, SyteLine, and MCP
prerequisites: Learner is authenticated to GitHub Copilot CLI.
Learner has access to at least one safe training or development repo.
Learner understands that the web app guides, the CLI works, and the developer verifies.
summary: GitHub Copilot CLI is the command-line workbench used throughout this course.
In Module 1, you learned the mindset:
In this module, you learn the basic CLI mechanics needed to work safely and consistently:
- capturing session notes in a markdown artifact
This module also introduces the fundamentals development loop used throughout Section 1:
Research -> Planning -> Creative detail -> Building -> Review -> Update skills, agents, and instructions
You will use lightweight repo orientation cues only:
This module is still read-only. The goal is to learn how to operate Copilot CLI safely before asking it to make code changes.
whyItMatters: GitHub Copilot CLI is powerful because it runs in the developer's terminal, close to the repo, shell, Git workflow, and local development environment.
That also means mistakes can have real local impact.
Before using Copilot CLI for research, planning, testing, or implementation, developers need consistent habits:
5. Avoid broad or destructive shell commands.
7. Know which commands are informational and which may change the repo.
This module gives developers the basic operating model for safe CLI use.
howItFits: Module 1 starts the fundamentals operating flow and teaches the CLI skills required before every later lab.
Module 2 then reinforces mindset and accountability for using those skills.
Section 1 fundamentals loop:
```text
Research -> Planning -> Creative detail -> Building -> Review -> Update skills, agents, and instructions
Where each Section 1 module fits:
| Module | Loop role |
| Module 1: CLI Fundamentals | Safe CLI operating habits and context control |
| Module 2: Mindset | Developer accountability and decision ownership |
| Module 3: Context Engineering | Build high-signal context inputs |
| Module 4: Research Before Planning | Gather evidence before deciding |
| Module 5: Planning Before Coding | Convert evidence to executable plan |
| Module 6: Creative Detail and Decision Review | Compare options and justify decisions |
| Module 7: Building with Tests | Constrain implementation with tests |
| Module 8: Agent Steering During Implementation | Keep execution scoped and controlled |
| Module 9: Review and Risk Gate | Decide GO / GO WITH CONDITIONS / STOP |
| Module 10: Update Skills, Agents, and Instructions | Promote approved lessons into reusable assets |
Repo orientation in this module stays lightweight: identify whether the repo appears LakeBank-guided, SyteLine-plugin-supported, or neither.
Deep LakeBank activation and full LakeBank flow are covered later in Modules 15 and 16.
Do not skip this module. Weak CLI habits lead to weak agentic development.
learningObjectives: Launch GitHub Copilot CLI from a repo terminal.
Confirm that Copilot CLI is authenticated.
Check Copilot CLI version and update state.
Explain when to use a fresh session versus continuing an old session.
Explain why stale context is dangerous.
Confirm the CLI is operating in the expected directory.
Use file references to provide targeted context.
Prefer in-session slash commands when available instead of restarting with CLI flags.
Use shell command interaction safely.
Explain what Copilot-related files belong in `.github/` and how that differs from plugin content.
Explain what `copilot init` and `/init` do.
Explain why `/init` should not be run automatically in every repo.
Recognize basic MCP and plugin commands.
Explain the Section 1 fundamentals loop and why each step exists.
Map each Section 1 module to its role in the loop.
Identify lightweight repo orientation cues for LakeBank-guided, SyteLine-plugin-supported, and neither contexts.
Decide when to continue fundamentals flow because specialized guidance is absent or unclear.
Identify Lake Companies plugins and explain the approved plugin installation workflow.
Use the Gists tab for ad-hoc context and explain when repo-based guidance is better.
Produce a read-only CLI session notes artifact.
keyConceptsMarkdown: ### Concept 1: The CLI runs where your repo runs
Copilot CLI operates from the local terminal.
That means the current directory matters.
A prompt that makes sense in one repo may be dangerous or meaningless in another repo.
Before asking Copilot to inspect, plan, or change anything, confirm:
- Which repo am I in?
- Which branch am I on?
- Is my working tree clean?
- Is this a safe training branch?
- Am I allowed to create files?
- Am I allowed to change source code?
For Module 1, source code changes are not allowed.
### Concept 2: What context is and where it comes from
When you interact with Copilot CLI, it builds a picture of the current situation from multiple sources at once. That combined picture is called **context**.
Context includes everything Copilot can see and remember at a given moment:
| Context source | What it contributes |
| Conversation history | The prompts and responses so far in this session |
| File references (`@file`) | The exact content of files you explicitly included |
| Current working directory | The repo Copilot is running in |
| Custom instructions (`.github/copilot-instructions.md`) | Always-on rules and guidance for this repo |
| Prompt files (`.github/prompts/`) | Reusable prompt templates the developer invokes |
| Skills and agents (from plugins) | Specialized workflow knowledge loaded when relevant |
| MCP server results | Live data from external tools (SQL, VersionOne, Power-Flex) |
| System prompt | Copilot's built-in behavior and any tool-specific framing |
Context quality directly controls output quality.
If Copilot has good context — accurate files, clear instructions, specific constraints — it produces useful output.
If Copilot has weak or missing context — no files, vague goals, wrong directory — it guesses.
The developer is responsible for providing the right context before asking Copilot to act.
### Concept 3: A CLI session has context that can go stale
Copilot CLI can use the context accumulated in the current session.
That context can help when you are continuing a task, but it can hurt when you switch tasks or repos.
Stale context can cause Copilot to:
- apply assumptions from the wrong repo
- continue an old plan
- refer to files from a previous task
- confuse old and new requirements
- overfit to a previous conversation
When starting a new training lab, clear or reset context unless the lab explicitly says to continue an existing session.
### Concept 4: File references beat vague descriptions
A vague prompt asks Copilot to guess where to look.
A file-targeted prompt gives Copilot better boundaries.
Weak prompt:
```text
Explain how this works.
Better prompt:
```text
Use @README.md and @docs/repo-agent-readiness.md as context. Explain the local development setup assumptions and identify any unclear steps.
File references are especially useful when the repo is large, old, or unfamiliar.
### Concept 5: Shell command usage needs guardrails
Copilot CLI can interact with the local shell.
That can be useful for:
- checking files
- listing directories
- running builds
- running tests
- checking Git status
- inspecting logs
- using safe read-only commands
But shell interaction can also be risky.
In this course, every prompt should be clear about what is allowed.
For this module, allowed shell behavior is read-only unless the learner explicitly permits writing the session notes artifact.
Safe examples:
```text
Check git status.
List the root directory.
Find README files.
Identify project files.
Do not run destructive commands.
Do not modify source code.
Unsafe examples:
```text
Delete generated files.
Reset the repo.
Rewrite config.
Install packages.
Run database update scripts.
Change source code.
### Piping other tools as Copilot context
Other CLI tools produce output that can be used as Copilot context.
Instead of asking Copilot to guess, run the command first and paste or reference the result.
Examples:
```text
Run this and use the output as context:
gh issue view 123
Then identify the acceptance criteria, unknowns, and which files likely need to change. Do not change source code.
```text
Run git log --oneline -20 and use the output as context. Explain the recent commit pattern and identify any risky changes in this area. Do not change source code.
```text
The file list from running: Get-ChildItem -Recurse -Filter *.csproj | Select-Object -ExpandProperty FullName
Use this to identify the project structure. Do not change source code.
This is especially useful when the answer is in shell output, not in a file — for example, recent commit messages, test output, build errors, or live query results.
### Concept 6: `/init` and `copilot init` are not neutral
`copilot init` or `/init` can analyze the codebase and create or update `.github/copilot-instructions.md`.
That can be useful in repos where custom instructions are desired and no instructions exist yet.
But many repos already have carefully managed AI instructions. Running `/init` casually can overwrite guidance the team intentionally put there.
In this course, do not run `/init` unless the lab explicitly says to.
### Concept 7: Know what belongs in the repo `.github/` folder
Before discussing extension layers, anchor the repo-level structure.
For Copilot CLI, these are the most important repo-scoped locations:
| Path | What goes here | Scope |
| `.github/copilot-instructions.md` | Always-on repository instructions for Copilot | Repo-wide |
| `.github/instructions/*.instructions.md` | Additional scoped instruction files | Repo-wide |
| `.github/prompts/*.prompt.md` | Reusable prompt templates for developers | Repo-wide |
Important boundary:
- `.github/` is for **repo-owned guidance** that should travel with the repo.
- Skills, agents, and hooks are usually **plugin-owned assets**, not normal `.github/` files in the repo.
This boundary prevents confusion: repo instructions define ongoing guardrails; plugin assets define reusable workflows and runtime behavior.
### Concept 8: Custom instructions, skills, agents, hooks, and MCP are the Copilot extension model
GitHub Copilot CLI has a layered extension model. Before using it, developers should know what each layer is.
| Extension type | What it is | Where it lives |
| Custom instructions | Always-on guidance text that Copilot reads automatically | `.github/copilot-instructions.md` in the repo |
| Prompt files | Reusable prompt templates the developer invokes by name | `.github/prompts/*.prompt.md` |
| Skills | A folder of instruction markdown, scripts, and resources for a specific workflow | Inside a plugin repo under `skills/` |
| Agents | A markdown file that describes a custom agent persona and capabilities | Inside a plugin repo under `agents/` |
| Hooks | Automation that triggers Copilot at specific lifecycle events (e.g., pre-commit) | Inside a plugin repo under `hooks/` |
| Plugins | A packaged collection of skills, agents, hooks, and instructions distributed as a plugin repo | `plugin.json` in the plugin root |
| MCP servers | External tool integrations that give Copilot access to live data sources (SQL, issue tracker, etc.) | `copilot mcp` configuration |
#### Lake Companies plugin catalog
| Plugin source | Primary purpose | Typical use |
| `TLCTools/LakeBank` | LakeBank workflow guidance and memory-bank governance for product repos | Product repos that follow LakeBank operating patterns |
| `TLCTools/CopilotSkillsSyteLine` | SyteLine/CSI workflow support | Customer modification repos with SyteLine-specific work |
| `TLCTools/CopilotSkillsPowerFlex` | Power-Flex workflows, script guidance, and conversion support | Power-Flex-heavy implementation and investigation tasks |
| `TLCTools/CopilotLakePlugin` | Generic Lake Companies reusable skills | Cross-repo tasks not specific to LakeBank, SyteLine, or Power-Flex |
For Module 1, developers should know what these plugins are for and how installation is performed safely.
Plugin installation should happen only in approved setup contexts (sandbox or explicit enablement tasks), not during normal feature work.
Workflow usage in LakeBank/SyteLine/MCP scenarios is covered in Modules 11-13.
Authoring them is covered in Module 14.
MCP configuration is covered in Module 13.
For the default Module 1 lab, do not change plugin or MCP configuration.
### Concept 9: Copilot CLI has three execution modes
When you start a GitHub Copilot CLI session, you choose how much autonomy Copilot has.
#### Default mode
Standard conversational mode. Copilot reads context, answers questions, and creates artifacts when asked. It does not chain steps or act autonomously between your prompts. You drive every step.
Best for: learning, exploring an unfamiliar repo, read-only work, drafting artifacts for human review.
#### Plan mode

Before Copilot takes any action, it generates a numbered execution plan showing exactly what it intends to do. You review the plan and approve before it proceeds. If you reject or modify the plan, Copilot stops.
In this course, the preferred interactive pattern is:
```text
/plan <task description>
Optional launch flag (same outcome, less interactive):
```text
copilot --plan
Best for: changes with risk, unfamiliar repos, any time you want to see what Copilot will do before it does it.
#### Autopilot mode

Copilot executes the task autonomously, chaining tool calls and steps without pausing for approval at each one. It uses your prompt, context, and instructions to proceed on its own judgment until the task is done or it encounters a decision it cannot make.
Start a session in autopilot mode:
```text
copilot --autopilot
Best for: well-understood, scoped tasks in repos where guardrails are already in place. Do not use autopilot in an unfamiliar repo or on work that has not been classified and scoped.
#### Modes control execution — instructions and context control scope
| Mode | Copilot autonomy | Best for |
| Default | Low — you prompt each step | Exploration, learning, read-only work |
| Plan | Medium — Copilot proposes, you approve | Risky changes, reviewing unfamiliar repos |
| Autopilot | High — Copilot executes without pausing | Trusted, scoped, well-guarded tasks |

Modes control HOW Copilot executes. Context, custom instructions, and safety guardrails control WHAT Copilot is allowed to do. Both matter. A well-crafted prompt with good context is just as important in autopilot mode as in default mode — autopilot gives Copilot more rope, not a different goal.
### Concept 10: Section 1 flow map and lightweight repo orientation
Section 1 teaches one complete loop before specialization:
```text
Research -> Planning -> Creative detail -> Building -> Review -> Update skills, agents, and instructions
Use this map while working through Section 1:
| Section 1 module | What it contributes |
| Module 1 | Safe CLI operation and context boundaries |
| Module 2 | Accountability and ownership model |
| Module 3 | Context quality |
| Module 4 | Evidence gathering |
| Module 5 | Build-ready planning |
| Module 6 | Option comparison and decision review |
| Module 7 | Test-constrained building |
| Module 8 | Implementation steering |
| Module 9 | Review and risk-gate decision |
| Module 10 | Updating reusable guidance assets |
Repo orientation in Section 1 is cue-based, not deep taxonomy:
- LakeBank-guided repo
- SyteLine-plugin-supported repo
- Neither (continue fundamentals flow)
mechanicsMarkdown: This module introduces the following CLI mechanics.
Start the interactive Copilot CLI session.
The interactive session uses a tabbed terminal UI introduced in June 2026. Tabs appear at the top of the session window:
| Session | Normal Copilot chat — the main working tab |
| Issues | Open issues in the current GitHub repo |
| Pull Requests | Open PRs in the current repo |
| Gists | GitHub gists owned by the signed-in account (public and secret) |
- `c` (on a highlighted gist) — insert the gist URL into the Session prompt
Issues and Pull Requests tabs exist in the UI but are not part of this course's workflow. The Gists tab is covered in this module because gists are a useful ad-hoc context source.
2. Use slash commands for in-session actions when available (`/plan`, `/init`, `/compact`, `/clear`).
3. Use terminal commands only when the action is outside session scope (for example login, version, update, or plugin setup).
This keeps context in one session and reduces unnecessary restarts.
If the learner is already authenticated, this may not be needed.
Display version information and check for updates.
Use this to capture CLI environment information in the session notes.
Download and install the latest version.
Do not require learners to update during the lab unless your team wants all developers on a specific version.
Open quick help from an empty prompt.
Display help topics from the shell.
Analyze the codebase and create or update `.github/copilot-instructions.md`.
Do not run this unless a lab explicitly asks for it.
Do not add, remove, or change MCP configuration during Module 1.
Do not change plugins casually. Install, uninstall, or update plugins only in approved setup contexts.
Lake Companies plugin sources used in this course:
4. Update an installed plugin when approved:
5. If installation was done in error, remove it:
For the default Module 1 lab, do not install plugins. Use this workflow only when a setup exercise explicitly asks for installation.
Summarize and compress the current conversation context to free up space for more tokens.
- A session has been running a long time.
- Responses are getting shorter or less accurate.
- You want to summarize the session so far before starting a new topic.
`/compact` is safe to use. It does not change source code or files.
After `/compact`, Copilot's memory of earlier turns is compressed into a summary. Provide fresh file references or context if Copilot seems to have lost specifics.

Remove all conversation history and start a completely fresh session. Unlike `/compact`, which summarizes and keeps a compressed version of history, `/clear` removes everything — Copilot starts with no memory of the current session.
- Starting a completely different topic or task in the same terminal window.
- Context has drifted so far that a compressed summary would still mislead.
- You want to verify Copilot's response with only the files you explicitly provide — no prior conversation influencing the answer.
- Beginning a new module lab where the previous lab's context should not carry over.
`/clear` is safe to use. It does not change source code or files.
| Command | What it keeps | When to choose it |
| `/compact` | A summary of the conversation | Session is long but context is still relevant |
| `/clear` | Nothing — completely fresh | Context is stale, irrelevant, or you want a clean slate |
GitHub Copilot uses token caching to reduce cost and latency for repeated context.
Developers do not need to manage caching manually.
- Large files referenced repeatedly may benefit from caching.
- Caching is most effective when the same context is used across multiple prompts in a session.
- Caching is a performance optimization, not a safety mechanism.
- Do not rely on caching to avoid re-providing important context.
The cache is invalidated — meaning Copilot must re-process context from scratch — when any of these happen:
- The session ends or is reset.
- `/compact` is used (the conversation history is compressed and re-summarized).
- A referenced file's content changes on disk.
- The model version changes (e.g., after a Copilot update).
- Plugin or MCP configuration changes (these affect what Copilot sees as its working context).
- Context overflows the context window and older content is dropped.

If a session has been running a long time, or if files have changed since they were last referenced, explicitly re-reference important files rather than assuming Copilot still has them cached accurately.
For this course, treat `/compact` and session resets as intentional acts, not automatic behaviors.
Include file contents in the context.
Use file references when the prompt should be grounded in specific files.
Execute a command in the local shell, bypassing Copilot.
Enter shell mode for running multiple shell commands in sequence.
Only use shell behavior for safe read-only commands in this module.
GitHub Gists are single-file or multi-file snippets hosted on GitHub. The Copilot CLI Gists tab makes them available as ad-hoc context without leaving the terminal.
1. In an active session, press `Tab` to navigate to the Gists tab.
2. Highlight a gist with the arrow keys.
3. Press `c` — Copilot inserts the gist URL into the Session prompt.
4. Switch back to Session and complete your prompt, for example:
Use the gist I just referenced as a checklist while reviewing the current implementation.
Summarize this gist and apply it as guidance for this repo.
- Debugging recipes and SQL investigation scripts
- Review templates (e.g., Clean Architecture checklist, legacy extraction prompt)
- API examples and code snippets you reach for often
When NOT to use gists for team guidance:
Gists are personal and not version-controlled with the project. For context that should consistently guide the agent on a shared repo, use repo-based alternatives instead:
| `skills/` in a plugin | Structured reusable workflow |
Gists are the right choice for personal snippets and quick one-off context. They are not a replacement for repo-based instructions or durable memory.
Before every Copilot CLI training lab, complete this checklist:
- [ ] I opened a terminal in the target repo.
- [ ] I checked whether the working tree is clean.
- [ ] I know whether source changes are allowed.
- [ ] I know whether writing markdown artifacts is allowed.
- [ ] I cleared stale context or started a fresh session if needed.
- [ ] I know the expected artifact for the lab.
- [ ] I know what Copilot is not allowed to do.
instructorDemoMarkdown: Show developers how to start a CLI session safely before asking Copilot to inspect or change anything.
## Demo step 1: open the repo terminal
Instructor opens a terminal at the repo root.
- Why a random terminal location can mislead the agent.
## Demo step 2: check the local state
Run safe shell commands outside or inside the CLI as appropriate:
- Are we allowed to write markdown artifacts?
## Demo step 4: check help and version
- Why course instructions may need updates as CLI features evolve.
## Demo step 5: show targeted file context
Use @README.md as context. Summarize the repo setup steps. Do not change files.
If `docs/repo-agent-readiness.md` exists from Module 1, also show:
Use @docs/repo-agent-readiness.md as context. Summarize what this file says about repo type, AI support, and risks before coding.
- The developer should still verify the summary.
## Demo step 6: explain `/init` without running it
- It should not be run casually.
- Some repos have carefully managed AI instructions that should not be overwritten.
- Only run `/init` when a lab explicitly asks for it.
- whether source changes are allowed in this module
- what I learned about safe CLI startup
- what I should verify before future CLI work

After reviewing, demonstrate that the CLI session is a conversation — not a one-shot tool. Ask follow-up questions out loud, showing learners they can interrogate Copilot exactly like they would a colleague:
What commands did you run to find the repo path?
Why did you mark source changes as not allowed?
How did you determine the Copilot CLI version?
- Copilot can explain its own reasoning. If a result looks wrong or surprising, ask why.
- You can ask what commands were run, what files were read, and why a conclusion was reached.
- This conversation loop is one of the most underused skills in agentic development. Most developers accept the first output without asking a single follow-up question.
- Treat Copilot as a junior developer who can show their work — ask them to.

lab.title: Start a safe Copilot CLI session and capture session notes
lab.safetyDescription:
Copilot may inspect files and write this markdown artifact:

```text
docs/copilot-cli-session-notes.md
```

Copilot must not modify source code.

---
beforeYouStart: I opened a terminal in the target repo.
I confirmed this is a safe training or development repo.
I checked the working tree status.
I know source code changes are not allowed in this module.
I know the expected artifact is `docs/copilot-cli-session-notes.md`.
I will not install, enable, disable, or remove plugins unless an approved setup exercise explicitly asks for it.
I will not add, remove, or change MCP configuration.

prompts.label: Lab prompt
promptText: We are practicing GitHub Copilot CLI fundamentals for an internal agentic development course.
- Do not install, enable, disable, or remove plugins unless this exercise explicitly asks for approved setup.
- Do not add, remove, or change MCP configuration.
- You may create or update docs/copilot-cli-session-notes.md.
First, help me verify this CLI session is safe:
1. Identify the current repo path if available.
2. Identify the current branch if available.
3. Identify whether the working tree appears clean if available.
4. Identify whether docs/repo-agent-readiness.md exists from Module 1.
5. Identify useful files I should reference with @ for this repo.
6. Identify whether repo instructions, plugin references, memory bank files, or MCP configuration appear to exist.
7. Explain whether this repo appears safe for this read-only training lab.
- Files referenced or recommended for reference
- Notes about /init and why it was not run
- Notes about MCP and plugins discovered, if any
- Repo orientation cue check (LakeBank-guided / SyteLine-plugin-supported / neither)
- Safe next step after this module

prompts.label: Optional targeted file-reference prompt
promptText: - what I should keep in mind while using Copilot CLI

expectedContents: repo orientation cue check (LakeBank-guided / SyteLine-plugin-supported / neither)

verificationChecklist: Did Copilot avoid source code changes?
Did Copilot avoid unapproved plugin changes?
Did Copilot avoid MCP configuration changes?
Did Copilot document the Section 1 fundamentals loop?
Did Copilot map where Module 1 fits in the loop?
Did Copilot capture lightweight repo orientation cues without forcing deep classification?
Did Copilot clearly identify the repo path or label it unknown?
Did Copilot clearly identify the branch or label it unknown?
Did Copilot clearly identify working tree status or label it unknown?
Did Copilot recommend useful file references?
Did the generated notes accurately reflect what happened?

evidence.summaryPrompt: Write 3-5 sentences describing what you learned about using Copilot CLI safely in this repo.
reflectionQuestions: What command or shortcut did you find most useful?
Can you restate the Section 1 loop from memory?
Did Copilot identify the correct repo and branch?
Which repo orientation context appears most likely right now (LakeBank-guided, SyteLine-plugin-supported, or neither)?
Did Copilot recommend useful files to reference with `@`?
Did Copilot avoid `/init`, unapproved plugin changes, and MCP changes?
What should you verify before starting a future Copilot CLI task?

skillCheck.question: Why is it important to confirm the current working directory before using Copilot CLI?
skillCheck.expectedAnswer: The current working directory determines which repo and files Copilot is operating near. If the CLI is started in the wrong folder, Copilot may inspect the wrong files, make wrong assumptions, or perform work in the wrong repo.

skillCheck.question: What is the risk of stale session context?
skillCheck.expectedAnswer: Stale context can cause Copilot to use assumptions, plans, files, or requirements from a previous task or repo. This can lead to incorrect recommendations or unsafe changes.

skillCheck.question: When should a learner run `/init` or `copilot init` in this course?
skillCheck.expectedAnswer: Only when a lab explicitly instructs them to. `/init` can create or update `.github/copilot-instructions.md`, which changes the repo's AI instructions. Running it casually in a repo that already has instructions set up could overwrite guidance the team intentionally put there. Only run it when a lab specifically asks for it.

skillCheck.question: What does `@ FILENAME` do in Copilot CLI?
skillCheck.expectedAnswer: It includes the referenced file contents in the context so Copilot can answer or act based on a specific file instead of guessing.

skillCheck.question: When is plugin installation appropriate in Module 1?
skillCheck.expectedAnswer: Only in an approved setup exercise (safe repo or sandbox) with explicit instruction. Routine Module 2 work should avoid unapproved plugin and MCP changes. If installation is performed, verify it with `/plugin list`.

skillCheck.question: What should a safe Module 1 artifact contain?
skillCheck.expectedAnswer: It should contain session notes such as repo path, branch, working tree status, source change permission, expected artifact, files referenced, commands or shortcuts used, `/init` warning, MCP/plugin observations, assumptions, unknowns, and safe next step.

summaryMarkdown: In this module, you learned the basic Copilot CLI operating model.
You should now understand how to:
- prefer slash commands in-session when available
- recognize MCP and plugin configuration areas, including approved install workflow
The goal is not to memorize every command.
The goal is to develop safe habits before using Copilot CLI for larger agentic development workflows.
readinessChecklist: I can launch Copilot CLI from a repo terminal.
I can explain why the current directory matters.
I can check or confirm the current branch.
I can explain why stale context is dangerous.
I can use file references with `@`.
I can prefer slash commands in-session when available instead of relaunching with flags.
I can use the Gists tab for ad-hoc context and know when to prefer repo-based guidance.
I can explain why `/init` should not be run casually.
I can identify Lake Companies plugins and explain the approved install and verification workflow.
I can verify that Copilot did not make source changes.
I can explain what I would check before starting future CLI work.
commonMistakes: Starting Copilot CLI in the wrong folder.
Forgetting to check the working tree.
Reusing stale context from another task.
Asking vague questions without file references.
Restarting sessions with CLI flags for actions that can be done with slash commands.
Running `/init` without realizing it can change repo instructions.
Installing or changing plugins without approval or outside setup exercises.
Letting Copilot run broad shell commands.
Trusting a CLI summary without checking files.
review.nextModuleTeaser:
Module 1: Agentic Development Mindset

Next you will anchor CLI mechanics in the human-in-the-loop ownership model.

You will practice how the developer stays accountable while using Copilot as an assistant.
